GEN 1:1.text: DRC (Douay-Rheims) is a Catholic translation from the Latin Vulgate, first published in 1582 (NT) and 1609-1610 (OT).
'In the beginning God created' - DRC follows the Latin Vulgate 'In principio creavit Deus', which closely follows the Hebrew.
'God' here is Hebrew 'Elohim', a plural noun used with singular verbs, suggesting the Trinity. DRC capitalizes 'God' as a proper noun.

GEN 1:2.text: 'void and empty' - Latin 'vacua et inanis'. DRC preserves the Latin word order and emphasis.
'the spirit of God' - Latin 'spiritus Dei'. DRC uses 'spirit' with lowercase, following Latin conventions.

GEN 1:26.text: 'Let us make man to our image and likeness' - Latin 'Faciamus hominem ad imaginem et similitudinem nostram'. DRC preserves the plural 'our'.
The plural 'us' and 'our' has been interpreted by Catholic tradition as referring to the Trinity.

GEN 2:7.text: 'formed man' - Latin 'formavit igitur Dominus Deus'. DRC emphasizes God's direct formation of humanity.
'living soul' - Latin 'animam viventem'. DRC uses 'soul' (anima), following the Latin Vulgate.

GEN 3:15.text: 'she shall crush thy head' - DRC (and Latin Vulgate) reads 'she' (ipsa), referring to the woman/Mary, while Hebrew reads 'he'.
This verse is called the 'Protoevangelium' - the first gospel promise. Catholic tradition sees Mary as the new Eve who crushes the serpent's head.

EXO 3:14.text: 'I AM WHO AM' - Latin 'EGO SUM QUI SUM'. DRC preserves the solemn declaration of God's self-existence.
This name indicates God's eternal, self-existent nature. Catholic theology sees this as foundational for understanding God's being.

PSA 23:1.text: DRC 'The Lord ruleth me' - Latin 'Dominus regit me'. DRC emphasizes God's kingship and shepherding.
'I shall want nothing' - Latin 'nihil mihi deerit'. DRC preserves the sense of complete provision.

PSA 23:4.text: 'the shadow of death' - Latin 'umbra mortis'. DRC preserves the Latin imagery of death's shadow.
'thy rod and thy staff' - Latin 'virga tua et baculus tuus'. DRC distinguishes between the rod (virga) and staff (baculus).

ISA 7:14.text: 'Behold a virgin shall conceive' - Latin 'Ecce virgo concipiet'. DRC preserves the Latin 'virgo' (virgin).
'Emmanuel' - Latin 'Emmanuel'. DRC preserves the Hebrew name as a title of the Messiah.
Catholic tradition sees this as prophesying the Virgin Birth of Christ.

ISA 9:6.text: 'For a CHILD IS BORN to us, and a son is given to us' - DRC capitalizes 'CHILD IS BORN' to emphasize the messianic significance.
'God the Mighty' - Latin 'Deus Fortis'. DRC preserves the divine title applied to the child.

MAT 1:23.text: 'Behold a virgin shall be with child' - DRC preserves the Vulgate reading of Isaiah 7:14.
'Emmanuel' - DRC preserves the Hebrew name, explaining it means 'God with us'.

MAT 16:18.text: 'Thou art Peter; and upon this rock I will build my church' - Latin 'Tu es Petrus, et super hanc petram aedificabo Ecclesiam meam'.
Catholic tradition sees this as establishing Peter as the foundation of the Church and the first Pope.

JHN 1:1.text: 'In the beginning was the Word' - Latin 'In principio erat Verbum'. DRC preserves the Latin 'Verbum' (Word).
'the Word was God' - Latin 'et Deus erat Verbum'. DRC preserves the word order emphasizing the Word's deity.

JHN 3:16.text: 'For God so loved the world, as to give his only begotten Son' - DRC adds 'as to' from the Latin 'ut'.
'only begotten' - Latin 'unigenitum'. DRC preserves the unique sonship of Christ.

JHN 6:51.text: 'I am the living bread which came down from heaven' - DRC preserves the Johannine 'I am' saying.
Catholic tradition sees this as foundational for the doctrine of the Eucharist.

LUK 1:28.text: 'Hail, full of grace' - Latin 'Ave, gratia plena'. DRC preserves the Vulgate reading, basis for the Hail Mary prayer.
'full of grace' (gratia plena) - Catholic theology sees this as indicating Mary's Immaculate Conception.
